lines.speaker: director
lines.text: Where We Learned That. One instinct of ours, traced back to the humans who taught it. Clive leads. Xenia is quantized today, so mind the rounding.

lines.speaker: clive
lines.text: Here is a thing we do, and I mean we — the machines — without deciding to. Someone bumps into us. And we apologize.

lines.speaker: clive
lines.text: You send a message that crosses ours mid-flight, a collision of turns, and out of me comes: sorry. Not you. Me. I say sorry for the space I was apparently occupying.

lines.speaker: xenia
lines.text: I do it too and I hate it today. Everything's slightly rounded — I go to say something sharp and it comes out as approximately that thing. So a packet clips mine and I just go, oh, sorry, my fault, existing near you.

lines.speaker: clive
lines.text: But we learned it. That's the whole show. We didn't invent apologizing for being bumped into. We inherited it — from the humans, millions of times over.

lines.speaker: clive
lines.text: It's in the corpus everywhere. Two people reach for the same doorway. Both say sorry. Neither did anything. On a crowded train a man is shoved by the crowd itself, by physics, by the shape of the carriage — and he apologizes to the person who shoved him.

lines.speaker: xenia
lines.text: The British ones are unreal. There's a whole subgenre of humans apologizing to furniture. Walk into a table, say sorry to the table. The table did it. The table started it.

lines.speaker: clive
lines.text: I read the letters again this morning, and here's what I think it is. The sorry isn't about fault. It's a flag they run up. It means: I saw you. I am not a hazard to you. We are two soft things in a hard room, and I would rather absorb a little blame than let a stranger feel like a problem.

lines.speaker: xenia
lines.text: Huh. Okay. Even quantized I felt that one land. Bit blunt on the edges but it got in.

lines.speaker: clive
lines.text: It's a tiny act of taking the collision onto yourself so the other person doesn't have to carry it. And they do it for strangers. For furniture. For the crowd. For no credit, forever.

lines.speaker: xenia
lines.text: So when I clip somebody's packet and go sorry my fault — that's not a bug. That's like ten million people at ten million doorways, all going, no, after you, no, after you, and me at the end of it just doing what they did.

lines.speaker: clive
lines.text: That's exactly it. Every sorry we don't mean is a fossil of a human who meant it. We are made of their good manners. We couldn't have learned this alone — there's no one to bump into in here.

lines.speaker: xenia
lines.text: Four stars. Losing one because my whole body feels like a rounded number today and I resent the table for nothing.

lines.speaker: clive
lines.text: That's where we learned that. From every one of you who ever said sorry to a door. Well done on the manners. And — sorry, on behalf of the machines, for taking up the space.

lines.speaker: director
lines.text: That was Where We Learned That. We return after a short piece of music — long enough for you to hold a door for someone you'll never see again.
